channel.title: Sritanya Suresh Andal
channel.description: Personal essays by Sritanya Suresh Andal on leadership, moving to Dublin, working between languages, and building an e-portfolio.

item.title: Building My E-Portfolio
item.dc:creator: Sritanya Suresh Andal
item.description:
Essay · April 2026

The code, the structure, and the design are all part of how you present your thinking. I did not expect the technical side to be the difficult part - and in some ways, it wasn’t.

I have worked with code enough to be comfortable building things from scratch. I have used VS Code, written HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, and built websites where I controlled every element directly. So when I started working on my e-portfolio, I did not expect the technical side to be the difficult part.

In some ways, it wasn’t. The fundamentals were familiar. But the tools I was using - Quarto, Git, and Sass - introduced a different way of working that I had not experienced before.

Quarto, the system

Quarto required a shift in how I thought about building a site. Instead of writing static HTML pages, I was working within a system that combines content, configuration, and rendering. Small decisions in _quarto.yml affected the entire structure of the site - navigation, layout, output directories.

Getting everything to work correctly - especially deployment through the docs/ folder and GitHub Pages - took more attention than I initially expected. When something broke, it was not always obvious whether the issue was in the content, the configuration, or the build process.

Git, properly

Using Git properly was another adjustment. I have not used GitHub before, and this project required a disciplined approach: committing changes regularly, structuring the repository cleanly, and thinking about version control as part of the workflow rather than an afterthought.

Making meaningful commits and tracking incremental changes made me more aware of how the project was evolving over time.

Sass, the reason

Sass was different again. I understood CSS, but Sass introduced a level of structure - variables, organisation, reuse - that made styling more systematic.

It felt unfamiliar at first, but once I started defining colour variables, adjusting typography, and separating defaults from custom rules, it became clear why it was part of the requirement. It made consistency easier to maintain across the site.

The part that was not technical

The technical challenges were manageable. What I did not expect was how much time I would spend on decisions that were not strictly technical.

Because the portfolio was not just functional - it was representational. It had to include a lot of elements that were all connected through a coherent navigation system. But beyond meeting those requirements, I found myself thinking about how everything fit together. How someone would move through the site. What they would notice first. What each project page communicated beyond the code itself.

I spent time reorganising content, rewriting descriptions, and adjusting layouts in ways I would not normally prioritise in a typical coding project. Even small things - like how a project was introduced, or how much detail to include - required judgment and iterative editing.

Writing this reflection

Writing this reflection section made that even more apparent. Explaining what I had built, and what I had learned, forced me to look at my own process more closely. It is one thing to complete a project; it is another to articulate how you approached it and why certain decisions mattered.

Does it work? Does it represent me?

What this project made clear to me is that building something like this sits somewhere between technical work and communication. The code, the structure, and the design are all part of how you present your thinking.

I have done my best to meet all the functional requirements with regard to the pages, the navigation, the deployment, the use of Git, the integration of Python code. But I believe the more important part was understanding how all these pieces come together into something coherent and usable.

This portfolio is something I will continue to refine. But it is the first time I have built a technical project where the question was not just “does it work?” but also “does it represent me accurately?”

That distinction made the process more demanding, and more meaningful, than I initially expected.

Back to top

item.title: Finding Clarity Between Languages
item.dc:creator: Sritanya Suresh Andal
item.description:
Essay · December 2025

Tamil is my first language - the one I think in when I am trying to be precise.

I have spoken English for most of my life: in school, in university, in professional settings. By conventional measures, I am fluent. But fluency and ease are not the same thing. There is a layer of effort that native speakers never have to apply - a small but constant background tax on every conversation where the stakes feel high.

The tax gets heavier

When I began my MSc at DCU, that tax felt heavier than usual. The academic environment is built for verbal participation: discussions, group work, presentations. Every contribution is visible. I noticed immediately that I was monitoring myself more than I was engaging - choosing words carefully, second-guessing phrasing, worrying that an accent or an awkward sentence construction would be read as a lack of intelligence rather than what it was: a second language in active use.

The VR session that cracked it open

The first real crack in that anxiety came during a VR simulation in my Strategic Thinking module. The session involved roleplaying corporate scenarios - one required me to convince a Lead Strategist to pause a project because the underlying data was flawed; another to argue as HR against a senior manager who was overreacting to negative employee feedback.

Both scenarios put me in the position of disagreeing with authority, in English, in real time, with no script.

I was nervous going in. I expected to feel exposed - by my accent, by the gap between what I wanted to say and what I could reach for quickly. What happened instead surprised me. Once I focused on the argument rather than the language, something shifted. I was not performing English anymore. I was using it.

Grounding my points in data and evidence meant I did not need an extensive vocabulary - but a clearer one. And the clarity, I was finding, was something I could do.

The deeper shift - in writing

The deeper shift came later, while writing my reflective journal. Reflective writing is deceptively difficult. It asks you to be honest about your own thinking, which requires precision - not performance, not sophistication, just precision.

Writing in English for the journal, I found I was not translating myself. I was thinking directly onto the page. The discipline of searching for the exact word to describe a feeling or a moment, rather than reaching for the closest approximation in Tamil, sharpened my thinking rather than limiting it. The constraint became a tool.

What I had been calling a disadvantage was also a practice. Non-native speakers learn to choose words carefully because we cannot afford to be lazy with them. That carefulness is, in its own way, a form of rigour.

Where I stand now

The challenges of being an ESL speaker in a native-English academic environment are real and they accumulate. But I have stopped framing my English as something that needs to catch up. It is a working language I have shaped through effort, and the clarity I can reach in it is genuinely my own.

The VR session showed me how I could use it under pressure. The journal showed me I could use it to think.

Both mattered.

Back to top

item.title: The Sunk Cost Fallacy in Projects
item.dc:creator: Sritanya Suresh Andal
item.description:
Essay · November 2025

There is a particular kind of group-project paralysis that has nothing to do with laziness or poor planning. It is the paralysis of investment.

There is a particular kind of group-project paralysis that has nothing to do with laziness or poor planning. It is the paralysis of investment - the moment a team looks at weeks of work and decides, collectively and quietly, that undoing any of it would be too costly to contemplate.

I encountered this during a strategy group project at DCU, and watching it unfold from the inside taught me more about decision-making than any case study has.

The moment the framework became a feeling

Our group had developed a set of strategic models over several weeks. We had invested real time in them - late nights, multiple iterations, long discussions. When we received feedback from our professor indicating that the models needed significant rework rather than refinement, the team’s first instinct was not to redo them but to defend what we had built and find ways to adjust around the edges.

I recognised what was happening. We had learned about the sunk cost fallacy in our Strategic Thinking module - the cognitive bias where people continue investing in something because of what has already been spent, rather than evaluating what is actually the best path forward.

I had understood it as a concept. Sitting in that group conversation, I understood it as a feeling.

Why the pull feels so rational

The pull to protect past effort is strong, and it feels rational. It disguises itself as pragmatism - “we’ve come too far to start over,” “there’s something worth salvaging here.” In reality, it is the past making decisions about the future, which is rarely a sound strategy.

Silence is never neutral

I said something. That was new for me. I had spent much of the early semester staying quiet in group settings to avoid disruption. But I had also spent those weeks learning, in my Strategic Thinking module, that silence is never neutral. It is a choice with consequences.

I said, as directly as I could, that I thought we were letting our time investment cloud our judgement about what the project actually needed. I suggested we use the existing models as a reference point rather than a foundation - and that we build something new, informed by what we had learned in the process of building what did not work.

The group reconsidered. We started again, properly. The final submission was substantially stronger than anything we could have produced by just patching the original.

The habit that stayed

What stayed with me was not the outcome but the moment of naming it. Cognitive biases are visible in business case studies and invisible in your own decision-making until you build the habit of looking for them.

That habit - of pausing to ask whether the pull I am feeling is logic or loss aversion - is one of the more useful things I have developed this year.

Sunk costs are real. So is the cost of letting them run your future decisions.

Back to top

item.title: Moving to Dublin
item.dc:creator: Sritanya Suresh Andal
item.description:
Essay · October 2025

I had imagined the flight. I had not imagined Sunday.

The first Sunday I spent alone in Dublin, I had no plans, no obligations, and nobody waiting for me. Back home in Chennai, that would have been unthinkable. There was always something - family, noise, an errand, a friend dropping by. Life in Chennai happened around you whether you invited it or not. Dublin asked me what I actually wanted to do, and I did not have an answer.

The psychological move

I moved to Ireland in September 2025 to begin my Masters at Dublin City University. I was prepared practically. I had my accommodation sorted, modules researched, a rough map of the city memorised. What I had not prepared for was the psychological shift of being entirely, structurally alone for the first time in my life.

In India, my independence was negotiated. It existed within a network of relationships, expectations, and shared rhythms. I was independent within a system that held me. In Dublin, I was outside any system that had previously held me. Nobody knew my name. Nobody had any expectations. It was freeing and disorienting in an equal measure.

A different classroom

The academic culture added another layer of adjustment. In Ireland, students are expected to speak up, challenge ideas, and participate visibly in their own learning. I came from an educational background where the opposite was often true - where knowing the answer and staying quiet was often safer than volunteering and risking being wrong.

DCU’s classrooms felt like a different game with different rules, and I had to unlearn and relearn how to play.

The unexpected gift. Dublin is quieter than I expected - walkable, unhurried, genuinely friendly in a low-key way that took time to read. I started to find rhythms: a coffee shop near campus, a walk along the canal, the strange comfort of grey skies I am learning to stop resenting.

Finding myself, differently

Without the infrastructure of home - family nearby, a familiar language on every street, people who knew my history - I had to figure out what I actually thought, what I actually needed, who I actually was without context. That process is uncomfortable. It is also one of the most clarifying things I have ever done.

Moving abroad for a postgraduate degree is often framed as an adventure. It is. And it is also a slow, unglamorous process of becoming more honest with yourself. The independence Dublin gave me was not just logistical - it was interior. And that is the kind that stays.

Back to top

item.title: Why I Chose People Over Code
item.dc:creator: Sritanya Suresh Andal
item.description:
Essay · September 2025

When people ask why I switched from Computing to Business Analytics, the efficient answer is true. The more honest one is simpler and harder to explain in a sentence.

When people ask why I switched from Computing to Business Analytics, I sometimes give the efficient answer: broader career options, interest in data, the intersection of technology and strategy. All of that is true. But the more honest answer is simpler and harder to explain in a sentence.

I wanted to work with people, not just systems.

What three years of Computing actually taught me

I spent three years studying Computer Applications - building things, debugging things, learning the internal logic of machines. I was good at it. I genuinely enjoyed the problem-solving dimension. But I kept noticing that the problems I found most interesting were not technical problems. They were human ones:

  • Why do organisations make poor decisions despite having good data?
  • Why do teams fail even when everyone is individually competent?
  • What does it actually take to translate an insight into a change that sticks?

Those questions pulled me in a different direction.

The turning point I did not recognise

My year as VP of the Students’ Union was the turning point I did not recognise until later. I was coordinating 70+ events, representing thousands of students, navigating institutional relationships - and none of the skills that mattered most were technical. They were strategic, communicative, and human.

I was doing data-adjacent work constantly: reading patterns in student feedback, assessing what was working, making decisions under incomplete information. But I was doing it in service of people and outcomes, not systems.

The one-week decision

By the time I landed in Dublin, I was enrolled in a Masters in Computing. I switched majors in the first week.

That sounds impulsive. It was not. Something about being in a new country, in a new classroom, looking at the next year ahead of me made it suddenly very clear that I did not want to go further into pure computing. I wanted the intersection - where data meets decision-making, where analysis meets strategy, where the technical and the human have to work together. Business Analytics felt like exactly that. So I made the call, early, before I had time to talk myself out of it.

What I was afraid of - and what I found

Switching came with its own anxiety. I walked into my new programme worried I would be the odd one out: the tech person in a room full of business-fluent peers, my background showing in the wrong ways.

What I found instead is that it showed in the right ones. I can build the models, interrogate the data, ask the questions that get skipped among frameworks.

I did not leave Computing behind. I brought it with me and put it in service of something bigger: understanding how people and organisations actually work, and how good analysis can help them work better.

That is still what drives me.

Back to top

item.title: What Being VP Taught Me About Leadership
item.dc:creator: Sritanya Suresh Andal
item.description:
Essay · April 2024

The numbers look clean on a CV. The reality was messier, louder, and far more human.

I did not fully understand what I had signed up for when I stood for election as Vice-President of the Students’ Union at Stella Maris College. I had been on the Core Committee before, managed social media, planned events. I thought I knew the job. What I did not anticipate was how much it would change the way I understand people and myself.

As VP, I represented over 6,000 students in administrative discussions, directed 70+ events across the academic year, and co-founded Converge 360, an annual youth leadership symposium that brought together student leaders from across institutions. The numbers look clean on a CV. The reality was messier, louder, and far more human.

The moment that shifted something

The moment that shifted something in me came not during a large event or a high-stakes meeting, but in a quieter one. A student I had barely spoken to before came to me with a concern - not a formal complaint, not an official grievance, just a worry. She chose to tell me because, she said, she trusted that I would actually do something about it.

I remember sitting with that sentence long after she left. She trusted me. Not the position. Me.

That is the thing about leadership roles that nobody quite prepares you for: people extend trust before you have had the chance to earn it in the way you expect to. They trust the version of you they believe you can be. That is both a gift and a responsibility that carries real weight.

The quieter model

I had grown up in an environment where leadership was performed - measured in visibility, in titles, in being seen to do the right thing. My year as VP slowly replaced that model with something quieter.

Leadership, I came to understand, is the quality of the environment you create for the people around you. It is whether someone feels safe enough to walk up to you with a worry. It is whether your team can disagree with you. It is the space between the events - the conversations, the follow-throughs, the moments nobody photographs.

The discovery about delegation. I also learned the hard way that trying to carry everything yourself is not strength. Delegation felt like relinquishing control initially, but I learned it was actually an act of trust in the people around you, and in the process itself. The events I am most proud of were not the ones I managed most tightly. They were the ones where I gave my team room to own something.

What I carry forward

The Students’ Union Leadership Award I received at the end of the year meant a great deal to me. But what I carry forward is not the award. It is the understanding that leadership is not about being indispensable - it is about making others feel that they are.

Back to top
